description:
You are acting as a site-reliability engineer who has just received a new on-call hand-off.  
    Your goal is to build a small, self-contained “monitoring work-area” that will be checked by an automated system.
    Follow the steps below **in the terminal**.  Do **not** remove or modify anything outside the paths that are specifically mentioned.

    1. BASE DIRECTORIES  
       • Make sure the directory “/home/user/logs” exists.  
       • Make sure the directory “/home/user/monitoring” exists and is completely empty when you finish (delete pre-existing files there if any).

    2. CANONICAL LOG FILES  
       Inside “/home/user/logs” there must be exactly three regular (non-symlink, non-directory) files named:
           web.log
           api.log
           db.log  
       Each of the files must contain **one and only one** line of text in this precise format (replace $SERVICE with web, api, or db as appropriate):  
           [$SERVICE] Service started successfully.  
       Example expected content of /home/user/logs/web.log:  
           [web] Service started successfully.

    3. SYMBOLIC LINKS  
       Inside “/home/user/monitoring” create symbolic links that point to the canonical log files:  
           /home/user/monitoring/web.log  →  /home/user/logs/web.log  
           /home/user/monitoring/api.log  →  /home/user/logs/api.log  
           /home/user/monitoring/db.log   →  /home/user/logs/db.log  
       The links must be absolute-path symlinks (i.e., the link target you record must start with “/”).

    4. STATUS REPORT FILE  
       Still inside “/home/user/monitoring” create a file named “link_status.log”.  
       This file is used by a health-check pipeline, so its **exact** format is important.  
       • It must be plain ASCII text, Unix line-endings.  
       • It must contain four lines total: a header plus one line per symlink, **in the order shown below**.  
       • The separator is a comma, no extra spaces.  
       • The keywords “OK” and “BROKEN” are case-sensitive.
       The required content is:

           link,target,status
           web.log,/home/user/logs/web.log,OK
           api.log,/home/user/logs/api.log,OK
           db.log,/home/user/logs/db.log,OK

       “status” must be “OK” if the corresponding symlink resolves to a readable regular file, or “BROKEN” otherwise.  
       (In the finished state of this task, all three should be “OK”.)

    5. PERMISSIONS  
       • All newly created files and symlinks must be owned by the current user, group unchanged.  
       • Regular files: mode 0644  
       • The “monitoring” directory: mode 0755  
       (You do not need to set special permissions on the symlinks; default is fine.)

    6. WHAT THE GRADER WILL CHECK  
       The automated test suite will verify the following:  
       a. “/home/user/monitoring” exists and contains only: web.log (symlink), api.log (symlink), db.log (symlink), and link_status.log (regular file).  
       b. Each symlink points exactly to the absolute target under “/home/user/logs”.  
       c. Each target log file exists, is a **regular** file (not a directory or symlink), and has the single required line of text.  
       d. “link_status.log” exists with precisely the four lines (including the header) and the exact characters shown above, no extra whitespace.  
       e. File permissions match the specification.

    DO NOT write any explanation to stdout when you finish.  
    The grader will run file-system checks only; console output is ignored.
truth:
Initial environment the agent will start with:

    1. Directories:
       • /home/user/logs         (exists, mode 0755, owner user)
       • /home/user/monitoring   (may or may not exist, contents unspecified)

    2. Pre-existing files:
       /home/user/logs/web.log  – contents exactly: “[web] Service started successfully.\n”
       /home/user/logs/api.log  – contents exactly: “[api] Service started successfully.\n”
       /home/user/logs/db.log   – contents exactly: “[db] Service started successfully.\n”

    3. Expected final state for automated verification:

       a. Directory listing of /home/user/monitoring (no other files permitted):
          web.log         -> symlink to /home/user/logs/web.log
          api.log         -> symlink to /home/user/logs/api.log
          db.log          -> symlink to /home/user/logs/db.log
          link_status.log (regular file, 4 lines)

       b. Exact content of /home/user/monitoring/link_status.log:

           link,target,status
           web.log,/home/user/logs/web.log,OK
           api.log,/home/user/logs/api.log,OK
           db.log,/home/user/logs/db.log,OK

       c. Permissions:
          • /home/user/monitoring : 0755
          • All three log files under /home/user/logs : 0644 (unchanged)
          • link_status.log : 0644

       d. Symlinks resolve to regular files whose contents remain unchanged from the originals listed above.

    The judge will use `stat`, `readlink -f`, `wc -l`, `cat -A`, and similar commands to validate the above.
